metadata.summary: Design-document practices from Google, Amazon, Uber, Spotify, Stripe, Meta, Microsoft. When to write, trigger rules, LLM assistance limits, common mistakes.

content.title: Design Docs at Big Tech Companies
content.summary: Reference survey of design-document practices at Google, Amazon, Uber, Spotify, Stripe, Netflix, Microsoft, Airbnb, Shopify, and Atlassian. Covers document names (RFC, ERD, 6-Pager, ADR), review formats, and the trigger rules for when a doc is required vs. optional. The core rule: write before coding; match document weight to change scope; always include "do nothing" as an alternative.

why.p: Design docs serve four purposes: early issue identification (cheap to fix on paper), consensus building, knowledge sharing, and onboarding. Without them, architecture decisions are re-litigated repeatedly and new engineers cannot reconstruct intent. LLMs excel at generating doc outlines and alternatives sections but cannot supply organizational history or system-specific context — those require human authorship.

list.item: Choosing a design doc format before starting a cross-team feature or architecture change
Adapting an RFC/ERD process to your team's async culture and scale
Using an LLM to draft initial structure from requirements (outlines and alternatives)
Onboarding new engineers via existing design docs as primary reference material
Deciding whether a change needs a 1-pager, full RFC, or no doc at all
Bug fixes and features under 2 days — skip the doc, write code
Purely internal team decisions with no cross-team impact
When organizational context, team politics, or proprietary system details are critical — LLMs cannot supply these
Post-implementation documentation — design docs must precede coding to be useful

section.name: company-overview
section.p: Design documents serve four universal purposes: early issue identification (cheap to fix on paper), consensus building, knowledge sharing, and onboarding. Each company developed its own format based on culture and scale.

list.item: Google: informal Google Doc, inline comments, emphasis on "why" and trade-offs, always include "do nothing" alternative
Amazon: 6-Pager (narrative prose, no bullets, max 6 pages) + PR-FAQ (press release first, then FAQ); silent 20-25 min read at meeting start; "working backwards" from customer
Uber: RFC evolved to tiered ERD — Lightweight (team), Standard (cross-team), Heavyweight (org-wide); mailing list circulation with named approvers
Spotify: two-stage — RFC for consensus (1-3 weeks open), ADR for recording the decision; break large RFCs into big-picture + focused sub-RFCs
Stripe: strong written culture; RFCs for any decision needing cross-team alignment; high-bar async review; mentorship integrated
Meta/Facebook: notably less formal — no company-wide RFC process; 1-pagers optional; compensates with high talent bar and long tenures. Not recommended for most teams.
Microsoft: FDD (functional) + TDD (technical) integrated with Security Development Lifecycle (SDL); security is first-class, not afterthought
Shopify: GitHub PR-based RFC with explicit review deadline; deadline-driven consensus works well with agent-drafted PRs
Atlassian: Confluence-based, triad model (eng/PM/design), validated against architecture principles

section.name: google-design-docs

rule.statement: Always include "do nothing" as Alternative 1 in the alternatives section.
rule.rationale: Sometimes the best decision is not to build. Forcing this option ensures the author justifies why building is worth the cost and risk.

section.p: Process: author writes → shares with close collaborators → wider review → optional formal meeting → doc becomes historical artifact. No strict format, but common structure has emerged: Context and Scope, Goals and Non-Goals, Overview, Detailed Design, Alternatives Considered, Cross-cutting Concerns.

section.name: amazon-6-pager-and-pr-faq

rule.statement: Write in narrative prose, no bullet points, maximum 6 pages; supporting data goes in an unlimited appendix.
rule.rationale: Dense prose forces the author to synthesize; bullets permit loose thinking. Every word must carry information.

section.p: PR-FAQ starts with a hypothetical press release announcing the finished product, then a FAQ document adding detail. This forces customer-centric thinking from day one. Meeting process: printed copies distributed, 20-25 minutes silent reading, discussion, printouts with handwritten notes returned to author.

rule.statement: Use the lightest template that fits the change scope; do not over-engineer the process.
rule.rationale: Uber evolved from simple RFCs to tiered ERDs precisely because heavyweight process for lightweight changes slows teams without adding value.

list.item: Lightweight: team-only changes, mini RFC, team lead approver
Standard: cross-team impact, full RFC, multiple team approvers
Heavyweight: org/company-wide, detailed ERD, principal engineers

rule.statement: Separate the RFC (consensus-reaching) from the ADR (decision-recording); do not conflate the two jobs in one document.
rule.rationale: An RFC trying to reach consensus and record the decision simultaneously produces a document that does neither well. The RFC is collaborative and iterative; the ADR is the immutable record.

rule.statement: Match document weight to change scope; write before coding, never after.
rule.rationale: Post-implementation documentation defeats the purpose — design docs exist to catch issues when changes are still cheap.

list.item: Bug fix: no doc needed
Small feature (1-2 days): optional 1-pager, team only
Medium feature (1-2 weeks): standard design doc, team + stakeholders
Large feature (1+ month): detailed RFC, cross-team review
Architecture change: RFC + ADR, company-wide
New service or system: full design doc, architecture review
API changes affecting consumers: yes
Database schema changes: yes
Security-sensitive changes: yes

rule.statement: Use LLMs for initial structure, alternative brainstorming, and prose refinement; require human validation for every technical accuracy claim.
rule.rationale: LLMs generate plausible but technically inaccurate design docs when system-specific context is absent. Technical accuracy is the one dimension where LLM effectiveness is Low-Medium.

list.item: High LLM effectiveness: initial structure from requirements, prose refinement, alternative brainstorming
Medium-High: risk identification, surface edge cases
Low-Medium: technical accuracy (requires expert validation)
Not applicable: organizational context, team history, stakeholder preferences, proprietary technology

rule.statement: Limit LLM-generated alternatives to 2-3 concrete options; agents over-generate when not constrained.
rule.rationale: 5+ alternatives overwhelm reviewers and dilute focus. The value is in comparing 2-3 realistic options, not exhaustively listing all possibilities.

antipattern.description: Using the design doc as a spec — conflating "why/how" with "what".
antipattern.reason: Design doc answers HOW; spec answers WHAT. Mixing them produces a document that does neither well and forces readers to untangle intent from implementation.

antipattern.description: No alternatives section — only the chosen solution is documented.
antipattern.reason: Reviewers question judgment when they cannot see what was rejected. Future engineers re-litigate the decision because the reasoning is invisible.

antipattern.description: Writing the design doc after implementation begins.
antipattern.reason: Defeats the primary purpose. Architectural issues caught post-implementation cost orders of magnitude more to fix than issues caught at the design stage.

antipattern.description: Too many approvers — more than 10 participants.
antipattern.reason: Consensus gridlock; the review process takes longer than the implementation. Google and Amazon both observe this threshold empirically.
Without the baseline, reviewers always ask "why not just leave it as is?" Include it to pre-empt the question and force the author to justify the build decision.

template.purpose: LLM prompts for generating design doc skeleton, architecture decisions, design review, and code generation
